column1: The four lines of the whole course — name each with its operation.
column2: Model = joint p(unknowns, knowns); inference = conditioning; prediction = marginalization; decision = argmin expected loss. Every method is a special case, approximation, or audit of these.

column1: A test is "99% accurate," disease prevalence 1 in 1000: PPV ≈ ?
column2: ≈ 0.0194 (about 2%) — a strong likelihood ratio (odds 0.0010 × LR 19.8) still loses to a rare base rate. Accuracy ≠ posterior.

column1: After a SECOND positive test (prevalence 1/1000, LR ~19.8 each), PPV = ?
column2: 0.2818 — evidence multiplies in the odds but is still below even odds; it takes three positives to cross 0.5.

column1: Coin with Beta(1,1) prior, observe 7H 3T: posterior mean vs MLE?
column2: Posterior mean 0.6667 (Beta(8,4)) vs MLE 0.7 — the prior's one pseudo-success + one pseudo-failure pulls toward 0.5.

column1: One PPV, three actions with expected losses treat 9.806 / no-treat 19.435 / retest 5.6846 — which wins?
column2: Retest (5.6846, lowest) — a posterior alone can't decide; the same belief justifies opposite actions under different costs. This is line 4.

column1: Plug-in bettor at price 0.68 over \(10^6\) $1 bets: net outcome?
column2: Loses ≈ $\(-13{,}235\) (−0.0132/bet) — plugging a point estimate where an integral (the predictive) belongs is a chargeable bet.

column1: Ridge coefficients vs the Gaussian-prior posterior mean: how close, and what does it mean?
column2: max|diff| = 4.5e-15 (machine precision): ridge IS a posterior mean with \(\alpha=\sigma^2/\tau^2\); your existing tools are special cases.

column1: Exchangeable-but-not-iid sequence: the running frequency converges to WHAT, exactly?
column2: A RANDOM limit \(\Theta\) whose law is the prior (de Finetti), not to \(E[X_i]\). Parameters exist because of exchangeability, not philosophy.

column1: Without-replacement sampling from N=10: pairwise correlation = ?
column2: \(-1/(N-1) = -0.1111\) — finite exchangeability permits negative correlation that no iid mixture can reproduce (de Finetti needs infinite extendability).

column1: For an infinitely-exchangeable sequence, what is the sign of \(\mathrm{Cov}[X_i,X_j]\), and why?
column2: \(=\mathrm{Var}[\Theta]\ge 0\): a mixture of iid can never be negatively correlated — the exact reason de Finetti requires infinite extendability.

column1: ECE 0.007 (honest) vs 0.089 (overconfident): what size confidence stretch produced the 13× inflation?
column2: A 1.6× confidence stretch → 13.0× ECE inflation. Calibration is where frequency and belief meet.

column1: The Pólya urn predictive equals which classical predictive, and to what precision?
column2: The Beta-Bernoulli / Beta-Binomial predictive, matching sequence probabilities to 1e-12; it physically realizes prediction = marginalization and seeds the CRP (M19).

column1: You random K-fold shuffle a time series — what assumption did you just break?
column2: Exchangeability across the split; the benchmark then leaks and reports optimistically low error. Exchangeability is an assumption you can be wrong about.

column1: Posterior odds = ? (the odds form of Bayes)
column2: prior odds × likelihood ratio; evidence adds in log-odds. The data's whole contribution is the LR — the prior is a separate multiplier.

column1: German tank, max serial 60 of 5 sampled: posterior mean vs MAP vs UMVU?
column2: mean 78.66, MAP 60 (the boundary — pathological), UMVU 71.0. On a bounded skew the mode is useless; report the mean.

column1: German tank: above what true N does the Bayes posterior mean beat UMVU on MSE?
column2: ≈ N=1075 (UMVU wins ≤1050, Bayes ≥1100). Risk is a curve, not a verdict; neither estimator dominates.

column1: Monty Hall switch-win probability: host KNOWS vs host RANDOM?
column2: 0.6667 (host knows) vs 0.5000 (host random). The bare event lacks its likelihood — the generative protocol carries it.

column1: At what disease prevalence does a single positive (LR ~19.8) reach PPV = 50%?
column2: prevalence ≈ 0.048 (~1 in 21). Below that, false positives from the healthy majority dominate.

column1: Sequential vs batch Bayesian updating — how different?
column2: Identical (to 1.1e-16): it is a running product, a running sum in log-odds — the linear-score-then-sigmoid shape of every classifier.

column1: Cauchy sample mean: how does its spread change as n grows?
column2: It does NOT — IQR ≈ 2 at every n (no finite variance, CLT fails); the median's IQR shrinks 0.6597→0.0069. A non-stabilizing average is a tail problem, not a sample-size one.

column1: Max-entropy distribution on \(\mathbb{N}\) given only the mean — Poisson or Geometric?
column2: Geometric (NOT Poisson); KL(geom‖Poisson) = 0.651. Max-ent is least-committal given the stated constraints.

column1: Is KL(p‖q) symmetric? Give the demo numbers and the consequence.
column2: No — 0.531 vs 0.737 on the coin, asymmetric and unbounded. Which direction you minimize decides mode-covering vs mode-seeking (M13).

column1: KL(p‖q) in words = ?
column2: cross-entropy − entropy = the bits wasted coding p with q; ≥ 0 (Gibbs). The SAME object is the training loss (M15), the ELBO (M13), and the predictive score (M17).

column1: A fair coin's entropy: how many bits, how many nats — and why care?
column2: 1 bit = ln 2 ≈ 0.693 nats. Code cross-entropy is in nats; state the base or your numbers drift by a factor of ln 2.

column1: Softmax temperature is the Lagrange multiplier of what, and what happens as T→0 / T→∞?
column2: Max-entropy over classes; T→0 gives argmax, T→∞ gives uniform. Entropy rises 0.822→2.125→2.322 bits as T climbs.

column1: Same 9-of-12 data: binomial p = 0.073 vs neg-binomial p = 0.033 — but the posterior?
column2: IDENTICAL: shared likelihood \(\propto\theta^9(1-\theta)^3\), shared Beta(10,4), mean 0.7143. The design splits the p-value; it cannot split the evidence. [S3]

column1: Is the likelihood \(L(\theta)\) a probability distribution over \(\theta\)?
column2: No — it doesn't integrate to 1 in \(\theta\) and its height means nothing on its own. Only \(L(\theta)p(\theta)\) normalized is a distribution.

column1: Fisher information \(I(0.3)\) for a Bernoulli = ? and what does it set?
column2: 4.7619; \(I=\mathrm{Var}[\text{score}]=E[-\ell'']\), additive as \(nI\), and it sets the posterior width \(1/\sqrt{nI}\).

column1: A survivor recorded as a death at c: what does it do to the MLE?
column2: Inflates the hazard — 0.6452 (naive drop/plug-in) vs 0.5376 (correct \(S(c)\) term). The fix is the right likelihood contribution, not a heuristic.

column1: Which ML losses are negative log-likelihoods, and of what?
column2: Cross-entropy = Bernoulli, MSE = Gaussian, MAE = Laplace; MLE = ERM under log loss.

column1: Exponential families are exactly the families (fixed support) with WHAT property?
column2: Fixed-dimensional sufficient statistics (Pitman–Koopman); \(U(0,\theta)\) escapes only by moving \(\theta\) into the support.

column1: The master shrinkage formula: posterior mean = ?
column2: \(\frac{\kappa}{\kappa+n}\cdot\)prior mean \(+\ \frac{n}{\kappa+n}\cdot\)MLE — the prior is worth \(\kappa\) pseudo-observations; the same formula is ridge, the Kalman gain, and partial pooling.

column1: 2-of-2 successes, Beta(1,1) prior: predictive next-success vs MLE?
column2: Predictive 0.75 vs MLE 1.0 — plug-in prediction is most overconfident exactly when data are scarcest. [S2]

column1: Posterior-predictive variance vs plug-in variance (Beta-Binomial demo): which is bigger, by how much?
column2: Predictive is strictly wider: 4.69 vs 2.34 — it adds the epistemic term (law of total variance). The plug-in 90% interval under-covers (0.8307).

column1: A/B test 57/1000 (B) vs 41/1000 (A): P(B>A)=0.9510 — ship which, and why not just read the 19:1 odds?
column2: Ship B — the expected-LOSS (regret) ratio is 81×, not 19×. P(B>A) is line 2; the ship call is line 4 and needs a loss.

column1: When \(\sigma^2\) is unknown, the conjugate predictive is Normal or Student-t?
column2: Student-t (df = \(2a_n\)), heaviest-tailed at small n; plugging in \(\hat\sigma^2\) gives thin Gaussian tails and under-prices outliers.

column1: Dirichlet add-\(\alpha\) trap: \(\alpha=10^{-4}\) with V=\(10^4\) categories gives what pseudo-count \(\kappa\)?
column2: \(\kappa=\alpha V=1\) — a "negligible" \(\alpha\) is secretly one full pseudo-observation. State \(\kappa\).

column1: Gamma convention trap: does gamma_poisson_update return a rate or a scale, and what does scipy want?
column2: It returns a RATE (course \(\mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha,\beta)\), \(\beta\)=rate); scipy's gamma/invgamma want scale = 1/rate. A silent swap corrupts every downstream number.

column1: Loss → estimator dictionary: L2, L1, 0-1, and pinball(q) each give which summary?
column2: L2 → mean, L1 → median, 0-1 → mode, pinball(q) → the q-quantile. Name the loss, then read the estimate off it.

column1: Gamma(2,1): mode, median, mean?
column2: 1.0000 / 1.6783 / 2.0000 — three different numbers on a skew; \(E[L2]\) at the mean = the variance = 2.0000 exactly.

column1: Newsvendor with underage:overage = \(c_u{:}c_o\): order which quantity?
column2: The \(c_u/(c_u+c_o)\)-fractile of the posterior PREDICTIVE (order 12 at the 0.75 fractile in the demo). Pinball loss gives any quantile.

column1: Lasso "sparse Bayes": is the Laplace-prior posterior MEAN ever exactly zero?
column2: Never — sparsity is a property of the MODE (MAP 0.0000), not the mean (0.1432, 0.3945). For sparsity + honest uncertainty use a horseshoe (M18).

column1: Credible vs confidence interval — the one-sentence distinction?
column2: Credible = a post-data probability statement about \(\theta\); confidence = a pre-data guarantee about the procedure over repetitions. Both correct, different questions.

column1: Prior-averaged coverage of a Bayesian credible interval = ? Does it hold pointwise?
column2: \(=1-\alpha\) exactly (0.9501, by Fubini); pointwise it VARIES (0.9715 at \(\theta=0\), 0.8827 at \(\theta=4\)) and degrades under a wrong prior (0.8288).

column1: Prior influence decays at what rate for an IDENTIFIED parameter — and for an unidentified one?
column2: \(1/n\) for identified (decisive at small n / boundary, negligible at large n); it NEVER washes out for unidentified directions — there the prior is the sole carrier of information.

column1: Jeffreys prior formula, and what is it for a Bernoulli rate?
column2: \(\pi_J\propto\sqrt{I(\theta)}\); for a Bernoulli rate it is Beta(½,½). Invariant to reparameterization — flat isn't.

column1: An \(N(0.5, 10^2)\) prior on a probability: what fraction of mass is impossible?
column2: 0.9601 outside [0,1] — "vague" on the wrong scale is confidently wrong. Always run a prior-predictive check.

column1: Flat = noninformative — why is a flat prior not coordinate-free?
column2: Uniform in \(\theta\) is curved in the log-odds; flatness isn't invariant. Want invariance → Jeffreys / reference prior.

column1: Haldane Beta(0,0) prior on all-successes data: what breaks, and how loudly?
column2: The posterior is improper — with NO error message. Prove propriety or use a proper weakly-informative prior.

column1: Sensitivity-fan spread at n=10 vs n=\(10^4\) (prior means spanning 0.6)?
column2: 0.3000 at n=10 (~half the prior span) → 0.0006 at n=\(10^4\). Draw the fan instead of arguing whether the prior matters.

column1: James–Stein vs MLE total MSE at d=10, \(\theta=0\) — the numbers, and the crossover dimension?
column2: MSE 9.98 (MLE) vs 1.99 (JS) vs 1.25 (JS+); the MLE is INADMISSIBLE for d≥3, no dominance at d≤2, crossover at d=3. [S4]

column1: James–Stein IS which Bayesian object?
column2: An empirical-Bayes posterior mean \((1-B)X\) with \(\hat B=(d-2)/\lVert X\rVert^2\). Admissibility forced frequentists to invent a posterior mean.

column1: The four Bernstein–von Mises conditions?
column2: Well-specified, fixed finite-dim identifiable \(\theta\), interior point, positive-continuous prior. Each has a named failure: boundary / Neyman–Scott / nonidentified / misspecified.

column1: BvM total-variation distance at n=5 vs n=500 (Gamma posterior)?
column2: 0.1668 → 0.0165 — the credible interval approaches the confidence interval as n→∞.

column1: Neyman–Scott (growing nuisances): the MLE of \(\sigma^2\) converges to?
column2: \(\sigma^2/2\) (0.4988) — biased, a BvM breakdown because the parameter dimension grows with n.

column1: Stein's paradox: does James–Stein improve EVERY coordinate?
column2: No — a coordinate far from the target gets WORSE; only total risk over d≥3 wins. Don't shrink a quantity you care about individually.

column1: MCSE formula — and how many draws for 10× more precision?
column2: \(\mathrm{MCSE}=s/\sqrt{M}\); error falls as \(1/\sqrt M\), so 10× precision costs 100× draws. Report it always — an unreported MCSE is a silent overclaim.

column1: Kong ESS formula — and is it cv² of normalized or unnormalized weights?
column2: \(\mathrm{ESS}=(\sum w)^2/\sum w^2 = M/(1+\mathrm{cv}^2)\le M\), cv² of the UNNORMALIZED weights.

column1: Importance sampling: a single run reports ESS 35% and "passes" — why distrust it?
column2: Infinite-variance weights: \(E[\theta^2]=1.693\) vs true 3 (44% low). The real alarm is the ESS FRACTION collapsing as M grows (62.65%→12.27%), not any one run.

column1: The tail rule for importance-sampling proposals?
column2: Proposal tails must be ≥ target tails — sample from the fat distribution to learn about the thin one, never the reverse. A Normal proposal for a t-target has infinite-variance weights.

column1: Rejection sampling: acceptance rate, and how does cost scale with dimension?
column2: Acceptance = 1/C, E[trials] = C; C grows exponentially with d (1-in-402 at d=10). This wall is what forces the move to Markov chains (M10).

column1: \(P(Z>4)=3.167\text{e-}5\): what does naive Monte Carlo at M=\(10^4\) give?
column2: Usually 0 hits (12/12 zero); relative error ≈ \(1/\sqrt{Mp}\). Importance sampling with a shifted proposal nails it — SE 84× smaller on the same budget.

column1: MH acceptance ratio — and why does the evidence cancel?
column2: \(\alpha=\min\{1,\ \tilde\pi(\theta')q(\theta\mid\theta')/[\tilde\pi(\theta)q(\theta'\mid\theta)]\}\); only ratios of the UNNORMALIZED target appear, so \(p(y)\) cancels. That is what makes Bayesian computation possible.

column1: RW-Metropolis optimal acceptance: high-d vs 1-D vs HMC?
column2: 0.234 (d→∞ product target, step \(2.38/\sqrt d\)), ≈0.44 in 1-D, ≈0.651 for HMC. A design target, never a convergence test.

column1: 16,000 autocorrelated draws with ESS 2304.9 — which number do you report, and why?
column2: ESS (~2300), not the draw count; the raw count overstates precision by \(\sqrt{\text{autocorrelation factor}}\).

column1: A single chain trapped in one mode of a ±5 bimodal: what do the within-chain diagnostics show?
column2: Everything passes — clean trace, single-chain split-R̂ 1.0028 — while P(θ>0)=0.0000. Four-chain rank-R̂ 1.7365 / ESS 6.1 catches it. Diagnostics are necessary, not sufficient.

column1: Plain split-R̂ vs rank-normalized R̂ on the fooling example — do they agree?
column2: No: 5.5422 (plain) vs 1.7365 (rank). ArviZ 1.x rank-normalizes — rank-normalize before comparing, or expect a benign gap.

column1: 2-state chain with \(\lambda_2=0.8\): relaxation time and integrated autocorrelation time?
column2: \(t_\text{rel}=1/(1-\lambda_2)=5.00\), \(\tau_\text{int}=9.00\) exact; ACF decays as \(\lambda_2^k\). Mixing is a spectral gap.

column1: Bivariate-normal Gibbs: lag-1 autocorrelation = ? (give it at \(\rho=0.9\) and \(\rho=0.99\))
column2: \(\rho^2\) EXACTLY — 0.8117 at \(\rho=0.9\), 0.9801 at \(\rho=0.99\). Read mixing off \(\rho^2\), not \(\rho\).

column1: Bivariate Gibbs ESS/M: formula in \(\rho\), and value at \(\rho=0.99\)?
column2: \((1-\rho^2)/(1+\rho^2)\) → ~0.010 at \(\rho=0.99\) (~100 iterations per independent draw). Blocking correlated components fixes it (89.7× gain).

column1: Gibbs is Metropolis–Hastings with what acceptance probability?
column2: Exactly 1 (ratio prints 1.0000000000) — full conditionals, no step size, no rejections, but only axis-aligned moves.

column1: rinvgamma(a,b) built from numpy's gamma — what is the exact call, and why the trap?
column2: \(1/\text{rng.gamma}(a,\ 1/b)\): numpy's gamma takes SCALE, so to sample rate \(b\) you pass \(1/b\). Convention referee-verified vs scipy.

column1: Complete-case analysis under MAR (not MCAR): what happens?
column2: Silently biased — \(-0.3959\) vs truth 0 (predicted −0.432); impute-within-Gibbs removes it (0.0149). "Just drop the missing rows" is a modeling decision, usually the wrong one.

column1: Rao-Blackwellize a Gibbs estimator: average what, for what gain?
column2: Average the closed-form conditional MEAN instead of the raw draw — here a 58.8× variance reduction, for free.

column1: \(N(0,I)\) in d=1000: where does the probability mass live, and how far from the mode?
column2: In a thin shell at \(\lVert\theta\rVert\approx\sqrt d=31.61\) (sd 0.71); ZERO draws land within \(\sqrt d/2\) of the mode. The typical set, not the mode. [S1]

column1: RW-Metropolis ESS/iter scaling with dimension, and iters per independent draw at d=1000?
column2: \(\propto d^{-0.94}\); ~2492 iterations per independent draw at d=1000, even optimally tuned. Random walks die in the typical set.

column1: Leapfrog stability threshold \(\varepsilon_\text{crit}\), and what a divergence physically is?
column2: \(\varepsilon_\text{crit}=2/\omega\) (marginal at exactly 2; stable 2.00, explodes 2.01). A divergence = local curvature exceeding \(2/\varepsilon\) — an UNexplored region.

column1: Neal's funnel: divergences centered vs non-centered, and how deep does v reach?
column2: 13 → 0 divergences; v reaches \(-1.97\) (centered, stuck at the neck) vs \(-10.01\) (non-centered). LocScaleReparam flattens the curvature — the reflex for hierarchical models (M16).

column1: HMC optimal acceptance — and is a LOW HMC acceptance benign like the RW 0.234?
column2: ≈0.651; NO — low HMC acceptance means \(\varepsilon\) is near the cliff and divergences are imminent. It is an alarm, not the healthy 0.234.

column1: SGLD discretizes which SDE, and why not trust its tail probabilities?
column2: Overdamped Langevin (Euler–Maruyama); at any usable step it is over-dispersed (variance \(1/(1-\varepsilon/4)\)), exact only as \(\varepsilon\to0\). Fine for point predictions, not calibrated tails.

column1: The ELBO identity: \(\log p(x) = ?\) — and is the KL to the prior or the posterior?
column2: \(=\mathrm{ELBO}(q)+\mathrm{KL}(q\,\|\,p(\cdot\mid x))\) — KL to the POSTERIOR. Since \(\log p(x)\) is fixed, maximizing the ELBO minimizes that KL.

column1: Laplace / EM / mean-field VI — each is what choice of q, with what signature failure?
column2: Laplace = Gaussian at the mode (ELBO-optimal only asymptotically); EM = Dirac on \(\theta\) (no uncertainty); mean-field = factorized (means right, variances shrunk).

column1: Mean-field VI on a correlated Gaussian: the marginal variance shrinks by exactly what factor?
column2: \(1-\rho^2\) exactly (0.36 at \(\rho=0.8\)) — reverse-KL is mode-seeking. VI is confidently wrong on interval widths.

column1: CAVI vs exact posterior-sd ratio for a NIG target — the closed form?
column2: \(\sqrt{(a_n-1)/a_n}=\sqrt{0.8}=0.8944\) exactly; mean-field misses the t's \(\nu/(\nu-2)\) tail inflation.

column1: Sum ELBO(q) + KL(q‖posterior) across DIFFERENT q on the same model — what do you get?
column2: A constant: \(-2.3979\) for every q (because \(\log p(x)\) is fixed). That is why maximizing the ELBO = minimizing the KL.

column1: A converged (flat) ELBO on a bad q-family — what does it prove about closeness to the posterior?
column2: Nothing — only that you found the best member of Q; the residual KL is invisible without a second method. A confident mirage.

column1: Ridge(\(\alpha\)) coefficients vs the Bayesian posterior mean — relationship, and \(\alpha\) in terms of \(\sigma^2,\tau^2\)?
column2: Identical to machine precision (8.9e-16); \(\alpha=\sigma^2/\tau^2\) exactly. The penalty IS a prior precision.

column1: Predictive variance in Bayesian regression: the two terms, and which grows off-support?
column2: \(x_*^\top\Sigma_n x_*\) (epistemic, grows QUADRATICALLY off-support) \(+\ \sigma^2\) (aleatoric). The trumpet flares where the data ran out — exactly what OLS's point prediction hides.

column1: Cross-validating \(\lambda\) (RidgeCV) is secretly which Bayesian procedure?
column2: Empirical Bayes — CV-\(\alpha\) and the evidence-maximizing \(\sigma^2/\hat\tau^2\) track each other (corr 0.795). Tuning the regularizer = estimating the prior variance.

column1: Three outliers injected: how far does the OLS slope move vs a t-likelihood slope?
column2: OLS 0.4942→0.9109 (84% move); the t-slope stays 0.5187 (within 5%). A t-likelihood is a Gamma scale-mixture that down-weights outliers automatically.

column1: \(\sigma^2\) unknown in regression: the predictive is Student-t with how many df?
column2: \(t_{2a_n}\), scale\(^2=(b_n/a_n)(1+x_*^\top V_n x_*)\); wider than Gaussian by the t-multiplier (1.076 at \(\nu=17\)), most at small n.

column1: How many features? becomes which Bayesian question?
column2: How strong a prior? — underfit/overfit = prior too strong/too weak, a continuous knob the evidence sets. The doorway to Gaussian processes (M20).

column1: Logistic MLE on linearly separable data: what happens?
column2: It DIVERGES (\(\lVert\hat w\rVert\to\infty\), loss→0, no finite minimizer; the 17.955 plateau is optimizer tolerance). Any proper Gaussian prior = weight decay cures it.

column1: A GLM = which three ingredients?
column2: Exp-family response + linear predictor + a link onto the natural parameter. Bernoulli→logit, Poisson→log, Normal→identity — one recipe, different response stories.

column1: MacKay's moderated prediction \(\sigma(m/\sqrt{1+\pi s^2/8})\): where is its gap vs plug-in exactly zero, and where largest?
column2: Exactly zero at p=0.5 (\(\kappa>0\) can't flip the logit sign); largest at small n and \(p\approx0.854\). Integrate, don't plug — the plug-in is overconfident and miscalibrated.

column1: Cross-entropy training IS what, statistically?
column2: Bernoulli/categorical GLM maximum likelihood; MSE is Gaussian GLM ML. Choosing a loss is choosing a response distribution.

column1: Survival regression, censoring 3%→70%: the \(S(c)\)-MLE slope vs the drop-censored slope?
column2: \(S(c)\) holds (0.5497 / 0.554 / 0.5335); drop-censored collapses (0.447→0.1628→0.0969). A survivor contributes \(S(c)\), not a density.

column1: Naive Bayes vs logistic: who wins asymptotically, and is there a guaranteed crossover?
column2: Logistic has equal-or-lower asymptotic error and wins under misspecification (NB plateau 0.204 vs LR 0.069); naive Bayes reaches its asymptote faster. No guaranteed crossover — a race, not a law.

column1: Partial-pooling weight \(w_j = ?\) — and it is which earlier formula, one level up?
column2: \(w_j=(1/\sigma_j^2)/(1/\sigma_j^2+1/\tau^2)\); M05's master shrinkage at the second level = James–Stein made adaptive.

column1: Eight schools: raw \(\theta_A=28\) shrinks to what (and what are \(\mu,\tau\))?
column2: 8.23 (\(\mu=6.49\), \(\tau\) median 2.80) — the noisiest groups shrink hardest.

column1: Bake-off held-out MSE: no-pool vs complete-pool vs adaptive partial pooling?
column2: 2.019 / 1.978 / 1.691 — partial pooling beats BOTH extremes out of sample (trades a little bias for a large variance cut).

column1: Centered vs non-centered eight-schools: divergences, and how low a \(\tau\) does each explore?
column2: 86 divergences, never below \(\tau=0.723\) (centered) vs 0 divergences, reaches 0.004 (non-centered). Every hierarchical model is a funnel — non-center by reflex.

column1: Eight-schools empirical-Bayes \(\hat\tau = ?\) — and what does the plug-in do to intervals?
column2: \(\hat\tau=0.00\) (a boundary estimate!); plug-in intervals come out 27% too narrow at J=8. Marginalize \(\tau\) when groups are few — EB is safe only at scale.

column1: A small \(\hat\tau\approx0\) with few groups — does it mean "no group effect"?
column2: No — only that the data can't rule it out; \(\tau\)'s posterior has a long tail. Report it, don't collapse it to a point.

column1: Posterior predictive check: is a PPC p-value a calibrated significance test?
column2: No — the data are used twice, so it is conservative (not Uniform); only extreme values inform, about the statistic you chose. Poisson PPC p=0.0000 → NB fix 0.611. [S5]

column1: Log-evidence peaks at polynomial degree 3 while training MSE does what?
column2: Falls MONOTONICALLY (0.4200→0.1656) — evidence has a built-in Occam factor (it even dips at degree 2, where a useless term pays Occam cost for zero fit gain). Complexity control by integration.

column1: Evidence equals which quantity from module 02?
column2: The prior-predictive density \(p(y)=\int p(y\mid\theta)p(\theta)d\theta\) — M02's discarded denominator; \(-\log p(y)\) is a codelength (MDL).

column1: Lindley's paradox at n=\(10^4\), \(\bar x=0.0258\): p-value vs \(\mathrm{BF}_{01}\)?
column2: p=0.0099 (rejects) but \(\mathrm{BF}_{01}=1.80\to17.93\) as \(\tau=0.5\to5\) (favors \(H_0\)), diverging as \(\tau\to\infty\). A diffuse alternative pays an Occam tax; the prior width never washes out.

column1: p = 0.049: roughly what replication probability at p<0.05?
column2: ≈0.499 — a coin flip. Significance is signal-to-noise, not a promise of replication (a 2-SE effect has power 0.514).

column1: PSIS-LOO vs exact LOO vs 10-fold vs WAIC on the same fit (elpd scale)?
column2: \(-41.76\approx-41.94\approx-41.63\approx-41.66\) — all agree from a single fit. Evidence for M-closed, LOO/stacking for M-open.

column1: Top hit of a large scan: raw 5.29 vs true 2.55 — what do you report?
column2: The shrunk estimate 2.80 (winner's curse: \(\hat A\) from \(\mathrm{Var}(X)=A+1\)). The single most-significant result is the MOST inflated.

column1: BH vs local-fdr at q=0.1: how many discoveries, and what are they estimating?
column2: 166 vs 174 (FDP 0.069) — BH IS a Bayesian local-fdr, \(\mathrm{fdr}(z)=P(\text{null}\mid z)\); both control the same posterior null-probability.

column1: Sparse problem, 5-of-100 signals: horseshoe vs lasso vs ridge RMSE?
column2: 0.033 (horseshoe) vs 0.061 (lasso, 35 false positives) vs 0.267 (ridge, no zeros). Sparsity needs LOCAL, not global, shrinkage.

column1: Misspecified model: posterior sd 0.0739 but true sampling sd 0.1046 — which quantity fixes it?
column2: The sandwich SE (0.1019) matches truth; the posterior is exactly right about a WRONG model. Internal coherence is intact, external calibration is not — audit the width.

column1: Power posterior \(p_\eta\propto p(y\mid\theta)^\eta\pi(\theta)\): effect on variance, and the \(\eta^*\) here?
column2: Scales posterior variance by \(1/\eta\) (exact for flat priors); \(\eta^*=0.526\) recalibrated the misspecified slope to its sandwich width.

column1: EB interval width ratio at J=10 vs J=1000 — is EB safe at scale?
column2: 0.8657 mean but ERRATIC at J=10 (worst 0.0013, the \(\hat\tau\approx0\) boundary) → 0.9989 at J=1000. Overconfident + erratic at small J, essentially exact at scale.

column1: EM responsibilities vs Gibbs labels: which flags more uncertain boundary points, and why?
column2: Gibbs (38 vs 24) — EM's responsibilities are plug-ins at \(\hat\theta\); Gibbs also averages over WHERE the boundary is. EM's crispness is partly an artifact.

column1: Averaging per-component traces without fixing label switching estimates what?
column2: A nonexistent parameter (raw \(\overline{\mu_1}\approx1.70\), the data midpoint) — the mixture posterior has \(K!\) identical modes. Impose an ordering (swaps 27→0).

column1: CRP expected number of clusters \(E[K_n]\): exact formula, and why not \(\alpha\log n\)?
column2: \(\alpha[\psi(\alpha+n)-\psi(\alpha)]\); the \(\alpha\log n\) shorthand errs in an \(\alpha\)-dependent direction (under at \(\alpha=1\), badly over at \(\alpha=5\): 23.587 vs 31.07).

column1: Parallel tempering with swap-accept 0.92 but the chain is still trapped at 0.013 — the lesson?
column2: Acceptance rate is NOT mixing; a timid ladder whose top rung isn't hot enough never escapes. Plain P(x>0)=0.000 vs a proper tempered 0.532.

column1: DP mixture truncated at L=15: what does the posterior over #clusters do?
column2: Peaks at the TRUE K=4 (p=0.482); stick weights decay geometrically so only \(O(\alpha\log n)\) sticks are occupied. L is a computational cap, not a belief about K.

column1: Is the Dirichlet process "infinitely many clusters"? Is it \(\alpha\)-free?
column2: No and no — it is discrete and parsimonious (\(O(\alpha\log n)\) occupied); \(\alpha\) is a real hyperparameter setting granularity (the booklet even puts \(\pi(\alpha)\propto(1+\alpha)^{-2}\) on it).

column1: A GP is which earlier module at what limit — and the kernel equals what?
column2: Module 14 at M=∞; the kernel \(k(x,x')=\phi(x)^\top\Sigma_w\phi(x')\) (Gaussian bumps → squared-exponential). The two routes match to 1.14e-14.

column1: GP posterior mean vs kernel ridge regression?
column2: Identical to machine precision (8.02e-09); the noise variance \(\sigma_n^2\) is exactly the ridge penalty \(\lambda\).

column1: GP marginal likelihood picks lengthscale 1.581 over an overfit \(\ell=0.05\) that fits training better — why?
column2: Evidence = fit + complexity (M17 in function space): best fit \(-12.412\) but WORST evidence \(-32.637\). Occam is built in — no validation set required.

column1: Latent-function band \(\mathrm{Var}[f_*\mid y]\) vs a band for a new observation \(\tilde y_*\) — the difference?
column2: The predictive band adds \(\sigma_n^2\); the latent band excludes observation noise. Calibration checks need the predictive band.

column1: An RBF GP extrapolated a couple of lengthscales past the data does what?
column2: Reverts to the prior mean (usually 0), confidently mean-reverting (band → 0.9834), pinching to ~0 at noiseless data. Encode a trend/period or it won't invent it.

column1: Exact GP cost scaling, and the standard escapes?
column2: \(O(n^3)\) (512× from n=100 to 800); inducing-point / variational GPs and tree-based BART are the escapes — three priors over functions, one inference.

column1: Kalman filter: predict step vs update step — which of the four lines, and what happens to variance?
column2: Predict = marginalize the transition (line 3, variance GROWS by q); update = M05 Normal-Normal conditioning (line 2, variance SHRINKS). Byte-identical to normal_known_var_update (8.9e-16).

column1: The Kalman gain \(K = ?\) — and it IS which recurring weight?
column2: \(K=P^-/(P^-+r)\) = data precision / total precision — the master shrinkage weight, now across time with q in the role of \(\tau^2\). Nothing new.

column1: 2-D constant-velocity KF with isotropic R and per-axis-identical Q/F: what shape are the position ellipses?
column2: Exact CIRCLES (axis ratio 1.000) — it decouples into two 1-D filters; along-track elongation needs BROKEN symmetry.

column1: An OU diffusion is exactly an AR(1) — with what coefficient (not Euler)?
column2: \(a=e^{-\theta\Delta}\), innovation variance \(\sigma^2(1-e^{-2\theta\Delta})/2\theta\); the filter's one-step predictives multiply to the marginal likelihood.

column1: Particle filter with N=\(10^5\) particles and ESS=2 — trustworthy?
column2: No — that is 2 particles with expensive overhead; ESS collapses toward ~1.48 within tens of steps. Monitor ESS/N and resample on ESS<N/2 (not every step).

column1: A particle filter that resamples every single step — what does that cost?
column2: Particle-path diversity (sample impoverishment), crippling any smoothing estimate. Resample on a threshold (ESS<N/2), not reflexively.

column1: Bayes action threshold \(p^* = ?\) — and when is it 0.5?
column2: \(p^*=C_\text{FP}/(C_\text{FP}+C_\text{FN})\); 0.5 ONLY under symmetric costs. 10:1 → 1/11 = 0.0909, 100:1 → 0.0099.

column1: The p=0.12 patient: a 0.5 rule vs the correct cost-based threshold — same action?
column2: No — the naive 0.5 rule sends home, the correct rule TREATS (expected loss 0.880 vs 1.200). A posterior is not a decision.

column1: Bandit tournament (arms 0.3/0.5/0.7): cumulative regret Thompson vs UCB1 vs \(\varepsilon\)-greedy?
column2: 19.6 < 99.7 < 212.4 (\(\varepsilon\)-greedy is 10.8× worse). Thompson = draw \(\tilde\theta\), play the argmax; its exploration shrinks on its own.

column1: Constant \(\varepsilon\) (fixed exploration) gives what regret growth; pure greedy (\(\varepsilon=0\)) does what?
column2: Fixed \(\varepsilon\) → LINEAR regret; \(\varepsilon=0\) locks onto a lucky-early wrong arm (0.42 of reps) — the overconfident MLE, sequential edition.

column1: EVSI is capped by what, and what does that cap represent?
column2: By EVPI = the current expected regret (value of perfect information); buy while value > price. EVSI(1000)=22.2 vs cost 20 — barely worth it.

column1: Complete-class theorem applied to acting: why is exploration not just a heuristic?
column2: Every undominated rule is a Bayes rule against some prior — you were always going to act like a Bayesian; the only question is which prior.

column1: A Bayesian is indifferent to which design rule but NOT which?
column2: Indifferent to STOPPING rules, not ASSIGNMENT rules. Randomization makes your written likelihood the true one: confounded \(\beta_T=5.288\) (17.5σ off truth 2) vs randomized 2.010.

column1: Power vs assurance — which is bigger, and why?
column2: Power > assurance (0.8013 vs 0.7140): power is concave over plausible effects, so averaging (Jensen) lowers it. Report assurance for go/no-go.

column1: Optional stopping: frequentist Type-I at 1 look vs 10 looks vs continuous monitoring?
column2: 0.05 → 0.2044 (10 looks, ≈4×) → 0.4664 (continuous, by the LIL). Yet the Bayesian posterior is unchanged and its prior-averaged coverage survives (0.9494).

column1: Optional stopping vs selective reporting — which one breaks calibration?
column2: SELECTIVE reporting (published coverage 0.6197); optional stopping leaves the posterior and its coverage intact. The first conditions on kept data, the second on an informative event.

column1: Factorial vs one-factor-at-a-time: information ratio, and OFAT's blind spot?
column2: D-ratio 2.000 (a factorial doubles \(\det X^\top X\)) and OFAT is BLIND to interactions. Factorial effects are just regression coefficients (½ the contrast).

column1: Permutation-test p vs t-test p on the Montgomery data — and what does the permutation test assume?
column2: 0.0311 vs 0.0313; the permutation test assumes ONLY the randomization-manufactured exchangeability under the null, not normality.

column1: \(E[Y\mid T{=}1]-E[Y\mid T{=}0]\) is the ATE only under what condition?
column2: Ignorability (randomization, or a correct + observed adjustment set). Absent it: wrong magnitude (4.704 vs 3.0) or wrong SIGN (Simpson \(-0.1428\) vs \(+0.10\)).

column1: You adjusted for one more covariate and the estimate got WORSE — what graph structure did you condition on?
column2: A collider (or a mediator): conditioning on it OPENS a spurious path. Truth 2.0: unadj 2.924, adjust{U} 2.001, add collider 0.504. More control made it worse.

column1: IPW with a minimum propensity 0.0071: the point estimate "looks fine" — what's the alarm?
column2: The ESS collapsed to 16.4% (a weight of 141); IPW is importance sampling (ess_kong = M09) and fails when positivity breaks. The estimate drifts to 3.427.

column1: Two DGPs with identical observed joint [0.3,0.2,0.2,0.3] — can the data pick the right ATE?
column2: No — ATEs are 0.200 vs ~0, and no test on the joint separates them. Identification is an untestable assumption; report a sensitivity analysis, not a p-value.

column1: A potential outcome you didn't observe is what kind of data, and what's its missingness rate?
column2: Missing data (M11); 50%-by-design. Under randomization \((Y(0),Y(1))\perp T\) (MCAR), so the naive difference is unbiased for the ATE.

column1: Confounder vs mediator: both are "do not adjust" — how do they differ operationally?
column2: A confounder left IN removes bias; a mediator left IN creates it. The advice only coincides for a descendant of T — draw the graph before choosing the regression.

column1: Which two deep-learning practices are EXACT Bayes (theorems), and to what precision?
column2: Weight decay = Gaussian-prior MAP (1.13e-07) and cross-entropy = categorical MLE (2.35e-08). Everything else is approximation / heuristic / open — the skill is the triage.

column1: A net calls 0.998 confidence; ECE single → ensemble → temperature-scaled?
column2: It delivers 0.851 (15 points overconfident); ECE 0.1741 → 0.1269 (ensemble) → 0.0433 (temperature scaling). A softmax probability is a plug-in at the MAP.

column1: Double descent: where is ridgeless test error WORST — the overparameterized regime, or elsewhere?
column2: At the interpolation threshold p=n (spike 4615.1), NOT the overparameterized regime (0.538 at p=240). A ridge prior of any width erases it (peak 1.001) — M07's flat-prior blow-up.

column1: Deep-ensemble spread — is it "the posterior"?
column2: No — a heuristic epistemic PROXY; it peaks (0.490) off-data where the data can't pin the model (M07's unidentified directions). Undercounts modes, ignores misspecification.

column1: Sampling temperature, annealing, and likelihood tempering — one knob or three?
column2: One knob (\(\eta\leftrightarrow1/T\)) — M18's power posterior in a decoding costume. Post-hoc temperature SCALING (Guo) is a cousin, not the identical lever.

column1: In-context learning rhymes with which Bayesian object — and is the real-LLM mechanism established?
column2: The posterior predictive on exchangeable data (de Finetti / M01), median agreement 0.0279 to Bayes — but the mechanism in real LLMs is OPEN.

column1: Marginalize \(\sigma^2\) (giving \(t_5\)) vs profile the plug-in: interval width and coverage?
column2: width 2.548 vs 1.943 (ratio 1.312 = \(t_5/z\) exactly), coverage 0.9508 vs 0.8942. Marginalize nuisances, don't profile.

column1: The two audits of the course — and which one agrees with the other, when?
column2: The posterior (post-data belief) and the sampling distribution (pre-data guarantee); BvM makes them agree in the nice case, the ledger is where they split. Run both.

column1: Conformal prediction's guarantee — finite-sample? conditional or marginal? what assumption?
column2: Finite-sample, distribution-free, MARGINAL coverage under exchangeability; NO conditional guarantee, and void under distribution shift.

column1: The status-column triage: of ridge, GPs, Kalman, k-means, bootstrap, deep ensembles — which are theorems?
column2: Ridge, GPs, Kalman = theorems; k-means and the bootstrap = approximations; deep ensembles = heuristics; the SVM hinge loss has no honest likelihood reading.

column1: Profiling a nuisance (plugging in \(\hat\sigma^2/\hat\tau^2/\hat\lambda\)) does what to coverage?
column2: It under-covers (0.89 vs 0.95 when marginalized) — the small-J EB trap (M16) generalized. Marginalize nuisances, don't profile them.

column1: The "calibrated Bayes" stance in one line?
column2: Bayesian for inference, frequentist for evaluation — run both audits.
